comment.body:
RV should be up when spot goes down. 
Okay, so ... the key takeaway from my post about volatility here is that volatility is proportional to liquidity. Bcs vol is the standard deviation of the price returns. 
RV usually goes down when spot goes up, 1) bcs returns are log scaled, 2) bcs plp are usually selling the right wing of the skew and buying the left wing, means buying downside puts, shorting upside calls... or in other term: shorting risk reversal. This is key bcs risk reversal is a synthetic stock trade, mainly bcs its PnL diagram is the same as having a stock position. 
In this case, customers are SHORT synthetic stocks. On a net basis of course. Why? - Bcs they hedge their LONG stock position in the options market.

comment.body:
Now this have a reflexive effect, bcs market makers who take the other side of the trade, short those OTM (downside) puts, and long the upside (OTM) calls.
so they are longer gamma up and shorter gamma down (positive speed profile).
Now, what this causes is that whenever the spot grinds higher, it faces increased liquidity as a result of MM hedging activity. They sell the rips and buy the dips when they are net long gamma.
This means, that whenever a plp are buying the stocks pushing the price higher, there will be increased amount of seller at those levels, as MMs have to sell. 
So the demand side must balance the supply side to the upside in order to push the price higher.

comment.body:
However to the downside, this is the opposite here, bcs to the downside there is lower long gamma exposure (shorter gamma), and at some point MM exposure even switches to negative gamma. 
What this means in practice, that: if the demand side is not enough on the stockmarket to rebalance the supply side from MMs (not solely them, but now lets keep it simple), plp stop-losses, margin lvls will be triggered and need to unwind their positions.
And as they sell, MMs have less and less long gamma, meaning, they have to buy those sell orders, but in a declining volume. So the liquidity decreases.
And when spot reaches MM firms net zero gamma line, they switch to negative gamma, where they have to hedge via selling into dips and buying into rips.

What this causes is, that MMs will be on the SAME SIDE that where the stocktraders are, so the counter-side, the opposite side who is needed to fill a sell or buy order is decreased... in otherwords the liquiditiy decreased significantly, and price needs to move a lot to find where sellers can sell their exposures.

comment.body:
Implied volatility is a different thing. Implied volatility is a bet basically on the ranges of returns into the future. This determines the risk premium on the options. Higher IV = higher premium, lower IV = lower premium. 
IV is basically the odds that the contract finishes ITM. 
Higher demand on a certain contract = higher premium = higher IV, and vice versa.

comment.body:
And look here: if customers sell upside calls, they lower those call IVs (selling vega / selling vol), 
and as they buy downside puts, they bid those put IVs (long vega / long vol). 
And this makes the vol smile steeper on the left wing, and flatter on the right wing.
So when the spot "slides" (on the x-axis) from the middle (ATM) to the left, meaning spot falls, the IV will increase, bcs we have bid IV values to the left.
But if the spot slides from the middle to the right side, the IV will decrease, bcs call vols are sold.

Now, but WHEN YOU SEE THE OPPOSITE,
meaning that the vol decreases to the downside, and increases to the upside,
it means that MMs are longer gamma down, and shorter gamma up.
Also meaning that the left wing of the smirk is flatter relative to the right wing that is steeper. 
Why? Bcs now customers are net long risk reversal: they are buying upside calls and downside puts = long upside vol, short downside vol.
Bcs they are shifting net short on the stock.

And this will reflexively increase the liquidity to the downside, compressing the returns, leading to lower vol, and decreasing the liquidity upside, leading to higher vol. 
Bcs dealers are the opposite: they long downside gamma, and short upside gamma.

comment.body:
When one build an algo for that, we build trees, so there is no exact answer, we have to consider many things. 
I will write posts about that.

But the two key aspect for now is that you have to understand the positioning, bcs that will give you a clue about the characteristics of the local environment. And you have to know the sigma lvls, bcs sigma lvs are like having the normal distribution projected on the chart giving you an idea about the probabilities. I have a special way to code it not simply IV/sqrt(252)
Sigmas give you an idea where the momentum is trading relative to the expectations, and give you an idea about potential price extremes, but you always have to take it along with the positioning.

If you don't algotrade, you have to be able to derive what kind of local effects you can expect from the positioning itself. And you compare to that what you see in the realized spot move in that location.
Sigma lvls give you a probability of how much probability is assigned that the price will remain in that certain range (so mean revert form the 'border'). 

When I personally trade manually, I like to watch the vol smirk itself, and like to check the intraday ATM IV (i made a script for that for IBKR), and compare it to realized intraday vol.

comment.body:
Lets say, market opens and starts to go down towards the downside sigma lvl. You see that there is local negative speed, meaning gamma is increasing. Momentum slows down, vol goes down.

Now, after the open, when traders start to take positions, vol is high, gamma is juiced. Plp are rushing into long puts, long downside vol, and the more the market goes down and down, they fomoing in even more. 
There is no real edge here anymore, bcs the IV is bid, odds are high on that downside trade. 
You have to wait.
You wait until it hits the threshold where you expect mean reversion first bcs it is an extreme, second, bcs the positioning says so. Vol starts to go down, traders start to sell their puts. 
But nobody is buying calls. Their IV is very low. 
You buy it, and market bounces. Your premium spikes. 

It is not always this simple, but this is the main idea of mean reversion trading. You buy, when the odds are low on a trade, not when everybody and their mom is buying that bet, bcs not good risk/reward. I showed it mathematically what it means.

comment.body:
It comes from the delta hedging activity.
If MM is long vanna = vol up/delta up, vol down/delta down for him.
You can have vanna flip around the middle of a local long fly pattern, or short fly pattern. 
The difference is that in a long fly pattern MM has positive vanna convexity, 
while in a short fly patter, MM has negative vanna convexity.

In positive vanna convexity, you have to see vol down in order to break through the zero vanna, 
while in negative vanna convexity, you need vol up. 

The reason is bcs in a long fly pattern, the speed is shorter to the middle, so is the vanna (to keep it simple). 
So mechanically you will naturally see vol up towards the center from any direction. This is what the vanna also dictates. 
Bcs if you move towards the center, MM becomes shorter gamma. It is below the center, MM has short vanna = vol up/delta down, 
but above the center MM has long vanna = vol up/delta up.
So whenever the spot moves towards the center, vanna forces MMs to buy into the direction of the momentum, and this increases the vol.

comment.body:
Have you read my post on Kelly? 
First, I decide how conviced I am, what porbability I assign to the trade, and what probability the Street assigns to the bet.
Then I check the positioning, bcs I don't want to be stopped out only if the momentum wanted to revert from lower. 
So risk taking is changing, but it is like 10% on avg for me on a single intraday trade. And if I miss, I take the loss, and revise my call. If still see that, I will take the position later. 
But I don't force it. 
The key is that I take position only if I actually got a read on the tape. 
No lotto and yolo shit

comment.body:
On intraday trading I usually take 0DTEs.
But for 0DTE I never take OTM, thats suicide for directional long vol trades. OTM 0DTEs are good for hedging and certain vol strategies but not for spot trading.

I use slightly ITM contracts bcs of the slower theta decay, and ATMs when the conviction is high, bcs ATM has the most gamma, so it gains value the fastest.
I use 0DTE if I look for a very fast trade, like what you saw today morning. 
And I use 1-2DTEs if I expect a sustained momentum during the day, and here, if I see good convexity edge, I may take 22/25 delta OTMs too, but the key is that here the contract is 1+DTE
Mind theta risk!

When customers go complacent, they don't hedge the downside anymore, this is short skew basically. They underestimate downside risk. 
We kinda see that today too, but not net short skew, but less hedging activity relatively.
Usually I look for lvls where they are expected to panicking back to long the skew one by other. This can be roughly derived from the positioning, CBOE datas, like where is the net exposure, simulating VaR, or different vol patterns (via stochvol models) and realtime simulating the reaction of the positions.

comment.body:
so negative charm convexity means here that there was shorter charm above and longer charm below, this is why theere was no delta decay from charm below my marked "charm flip" line.
But charm convexity is the same as gamma to time.
Logically: 
premium vs spot = delta
(premium vs spot) vs time = charm
(premium vs spot) vs spot = gamma
((premium vs spot) vs spot) vs time = ((premium vs spot) vs time) vs spot = color

so the gamma profile net lowered that naturally increased the volatility, but we were in a negative speed environment = shorter upside gamma, longer downside gamma.
also negative vanna. so vol up/delta down = MMs are buying, but there is shorter delta upside so less resistance, that kinda gamma squeeze-ish. 
This is why. The lvl mattered alone. That was the kinda line in the sand

risk aversion (bias towards downside protection), dividents, borrowing costs, supply/demand imbalances, early exercise features on american options, bid/ask spreads and transaction costs... 
parity is an ideal, reality is different.

comment.body:
theta risk, volatility, and strategy of course.
there was a trade when i chose friday expos, only bcs I was prepared that i will maybe hold them over the week, but due to the high price swings ,i don't see too much edge, bad sortino.
if i expect vol to go down (like yesterday, as MMs are longer downside vol), and we are in a MM short fly pattern, I mind theta risk, and I estimated that maybe I will need to hold those contracts very long, so the theta will kill the premium, so i went for a safer bet. 
With 0DTEs one has to be very accurate with timing. Yesterday, I felt that I can't time the reversal perfectly, so I chosed 1DTE.

I like OTMs for longer-term trades, cheaper and you get better convexity. But they only have extrinsic value, so the premium decay is faster.
For intraday trading I like ATMs and ITMs more, bcs ATMs have the highest gamma so it gains profit the fastest, and ITMs have intrinsic value that doesn't affected by vol and time. 
I use ATM when conviction is high, and use ITM when I know that I'm a bit off with timing, and use OTM when I go for longer-term trades...oooor when I'm 101% sure on a 0DTE intraday trade, but it is risky.
Also 0DTE OTMs are good for vol trading and hedging purposes for stock or futs positions

comment.body:
INTRADAY REALIZED VOL SCRIPT:

//@version=5
indicator("GKYZ Volatility", shorttitle="GKYZ Vol. Est.", overlay=false)


lookback = input.int(5, title="Lookback Period", minval=1)  


f_calcDailyRV() =>


    window          = lookback
    trading_periods = 390


    log_hoD = math.log(high / open)
    log_loD = math.log(low  / open)
    log_coD = math.log(close / open)


    log_ocD    = math.log(open / nz(close[1]))
    log_oc_sqD = log_ocD * log_ocD


    log_ccD    = math.log(close / nz(close[1]))
    log_cc_sqD = log_ccD * log_ccD


    rsD = log_hoD*(log_hoD - log_coD) + log_loD*(log_loD - log_coD)


    close_volD = math.sum(log_cc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    open_volD  = math.sum(log_oc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    window_rsD = math.sum(rsD, window)        / (window - 1)
    kD         = 0.34 / (1.34 + (window + 1.0)/(window - 1.0))
    rvD        = math.sqrt((open_volD + kD*close_volD + (1 - kD)*window_rsD) * trading_periods)


dailyRV = request.security(syminfo.tickerid, "1", f_calcDailyRV())


plot(dailyRV, title="GKYZ Volatility", color=#970ef3, linewidth=2)

comment.body:
so it estimates that your returns come from one of two hidden regimes.
defined by their own Gaussian distribution. I guess you have read my post about them. 
The means and variances tell you what the typical return and volatility are in each regime. For example, if state 1 has a mean of –0.00025, this means that when the model is in state 1, the typical avg return is –0.025% per observation. 
Then we have the variance of returns within that state. A very low variance means the returns in that state are very consistent, while a high variance would mean they fluctuate a lot.
The state sequence is all in state 1 means that the model finds no evidence of switching regimes during the analysed (1week, 1hourly candles) period.
And also we have a transition probability matrix, that tells me the probability of staying in the same state or switching to the other state at the next time step.

comment.body:
i had a put from feb19, i kept rolling a few portion of it, while taking profit out of it at the same time.
my entries (when i do manually, like what you saw in the p ast two weeks), are tied pretty much for the reversion lvls and the lvls i give you + intraday vol
okay, i have a machine learning based momentum metric, but i did a very simplified yet accurate enough indicator for you too, in order to get a better edge.

so the reversion lvls are basically the different slices of the probability distribution. it gives you literally a probability + risk lvl, that confirms or disproves the recent momentum.
then you have the positioning + orderflow. 
this gives you an idea about the dynamics that can be waited. 
longer gamma = slows down the momentum, absorbs intraday vol,
vanna flip lines, etc. 
and you look for anomalies at the lvls, and in the order flow to catch if the price doesn't behave there as it should, mechanically.

order flow can lie, bcs big players, funds like to scale into positions in small sizes in order to avoid catching others attention (like they did on tuesday) - i knew that they bought, bcs i have friends from here and there, and i just heard. nothing special.

if i wanna go for a reversion trade on the call side, i want convexity on the calls, and i want room for the momentum. i patiently wait for a certain lvl where the reversion probability is higher, and we get gamma on the calls to gain profit. like it was on thursday, i didn't rush into any trades.

know the instrument you trade with: option, futures, stocks, how they behave... know the underlying's dynamics how it interacts with others.
don't trade too much assets, learn a sector, or just one or too assets and master that. edge can be found anywhere.

and most importantly, you have to day up to date with current market theories in order to read the tape in the present times.

comment.body:
very different, bcs my algo determines the size of it, and its greeks... so it adds or/and decreases the portfolio exposures. 
like, I sold a ratio put spread 1:3 with March expo, it is basically a spot down/vol down bet, and as the put goes ITM, from vega and theta terms it is basically a risk reversal trade with long vanna, short vega, long theta, but the delta is negative. if i add long OTM calls, my vol and theta exposures can be mitigated, or even canceled out, but if the time to maturity is shorter, it won't affect the net vega and theta exposures... or if i chose an even longer dated maturity, it will have more vega impact ...+ their derivatives of course. so it is complex,

but for shorter term trades, one simply determines what is the bet: what is the timeframe you are thinking about and what is the vol that you bet on. long vol, short vol, beside long delta short delta bets.
and you manage the theta risk with lets say, longer maturity, but now you get more vega impact potentially... so it depends on the strategy.

comment.body:
What is IV? (in short)
It is the volatility risk premium on the contract. Indirectly reflecting the supply/demand of that contract.
Bcs the higher the demand for a certain contract, the more people are betting on that contract going ITM into its expo.
And if the demand grows, the price increases. Why? bcs the seller will have loss on that contract if the buyer wins. So he increases the price.
And bcs volatility is the SD of the logreturns, the higher the vol, the higher range of returns are expected on that contract. 
So the higher risk the sellers has that the contract will expire ITM. So he will ask for higher premia.

What we traders are interested in in terms of skew, is its relative changes.
You chose the tenor, or date you are interested in, and check how the skew looked like yesterday, 1 weekago, after the last OpEx, vs today etc.
And you can check where the sentiment goes. 
We usually meassure the slopeness of the skew by simply taking the 22 delta put vs ATM segment, or 5 delta put vs 22 delta put, and the 22 delta call vs ATM, and the ATM itself...
